summary: Blues‑heavy early set with scorching Garcia solos and strong organ
blurb: The Matrix show bursts with high‑energy blues numbers, highlighted by Alice D. Millionaire, Cream Puff War and One Kind Favor. Pigpen’s driving organ and Bill Kreutzmann’s relentless drumming lock the band into a tight groove. Standout moments include Garcia’s fiery solo on Cream Puff War and several rare deep‑blues cuts.
review: On December 1, 1966 at The Matrix, the Grateful Dead delivered an electrifying three‑set marathon that feels like a garage‑band jam turned psychedelic breakthrough. From the opening “New Minglewood Blues” through a raucous run of blues staples—“Betty and Dupree,” “Big Boss Man,” and the fan‑favorite “Alice D. Millionaire”—the band never loses its momentum. Garcia’s lead work shines on “Cream Puff War,” where his solo is described as stellar, and he adds a playful turnaround lick on “One Kind Favor.” Pigpen’s organ fuels the R&B feel of tracks like “Death Don’t Have No Mercy” and “Big Boy Pete,” while Bill Kreutzmann provides an almost “fourth‑arm” drumming intensity that propels the set. Though some recordings note occasional drop‑outs and a thin high‑end, the cleaned‑up soundboard capture (gd1966-12-01.sbd.32800.shnf) presents the performance with clarity and minimal hiss, making it the definitive listen for anyone wanting to experience this formative moment in Dead history.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings are positive and two mixed, with no consistent negative commentary. Multiple sources praise standout songs (Alice D., Cream Puff War, One Kind Favor), Garcia’s solos, Pigpen’s organ drive, and Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming. The overall tone is strongly favorable, justifying a +1.6 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Cleaned‑up soundboard with good overall balance, minimal hiss and captures the full energy of the set while preserving the iconic early‑Dead vibe.

key_highlights: High‑energy blues‑heavy set featuring rare early Dead material.
Garcia’s scorching solo on "Cream Puff War" and hot guitar throughout.
Pigpen’s organ drives the R&B groove; Bill Kreutzmann delivers near‑four‑arm drumming intensity.
Rare deep‑blues cuts like "Death Don't Have No Mercy" and "Big Boy Pete" stand out.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers scorching solos on Cream Puff War and Death Don't Have No Mercy, with a hot, pop‑toned lead throughout; his playful turnaround lick shines on One Kind Favor.
band_performance.Phil: Phil’s bass provides steady drive, complementing Pigpen’s organ and adding subtle melodic touches.
band_performance.Bob: Bob’s rhythm guitar is solid but occasionally less focused, notably on Me And My Uncle.
band_performance.Billy: Bill’s drumming is propulsive, with a near‑four‑armed intensity that fuels the blues‑heavy numbers.
